text: I mean "beside" on the concrete side of the metaphor.  :wink:  A physical bazaar is liable to be beside a physical cathedral — though the cyberpunk vertical arrangement would make for a nice illustration.

To my eyes it looks like I can/should just live in the first 128 colors.

text: Yeah, I knew it was a video and watched it :slightly_smiling_face:  I assume you are encoding everything with utf-8; the only sane choice? It may also be worth finding one of the 'complete' fonts that has all the unicode characters in it.  I think Google does one.

text:
Yeah, exactly, it's GNU Unifont, so there's room to grow there.

The input side is tougher. I currently deal in ascii, and I don't really have a good plan for supporting other keyboards.

text:
Elon Musk asks for suggestions on how to spend $190 billion. So among the 28,000 responses I'm sure you'll notice my suggestion, "Do a UBI program for open-source developers, so that any developer who is making free software can do it at minimum wage."
<https://twitter.com/elonmusk/status/1347356316763705344>

attachments.fallback: <https://twitter.com/elonmusk|@elonmusk>: Btw, critical feedback is always super appreciated, as well as ways to donate money that really make a difference (way harder than it seems)
attachments.text: Btw, critical feedback is always super appreciated, as well as ways to donate money that really make a difference (way harder than it seems)

elements.text: Elon Musk asks for suggestions on how to spend $190 billion. So among the 28,000 responses I'm sure you'll notice my suggestion, "Do a UBI program for open-source developers, so that any developer who is making free software can do it at minimum wage."

text: I like Alan Kay’s thesis that capital allocation is a skill just like programming or drawing. A good way might be to give the $ to ‘good’ capital allocators. That could be private investors, foundations, or heck even the NIH if you think they’re investing in ways that align with your values

elements.text: I like Alan Kay’s thesis that capital allocation is a skill just like programming or drawing. A good way might be to give the $ to ‘good’ capital allocators. That could be private investors, foundations, or heck even the NIH if you think they’re investing in ways that align with your values

text: Elon has a track record of his own of being a good capital allocator, albeit focused on private businesses

elements.text: Elon has a track record of his own of being a good capital allocator, albeit focused on private businesses

text: That's interesting. So a professional non-profit allocator group like VCs or Angels of the non-profit world.

elements.text: That's interesting. So a professional non-profit allocator group like VCs or Angels of the non-profit world.

text: I'm looking for a 3-D Legos simulator. Some 3-D Library where I can programmatically generate a 3-D scene of legos, and generate animations of those legos moving. I have limited experience using 3-D libraries. Does anyone have any pointers to libs or software I should look at?

elements.text: I'm looking for a 3-D Legos simulator. Some 3-D Library where I can programmatically generate a 3-D scene of legos, and generate animations of those legos moving. I have limited experience using 3-D libraries. Does anyone have any pointers to libs or software I should look at?

text: There's an official 3d creator called Lego Studio (made by Bricklink which was then bought by TLG). It can do realistic renders (not quite photo realistic but good), so you could certainly do an animation (painstakingly) with that.

elements.text: There's an official 3d creator called Lego Studio (made by Bricklink which was then bought by TLG). It can do realistic renders (not quite photo realistic but good), so you could certainly do an animation (painstakingly) with that.

text:
yeah I mean, depends what you’re optimizing for I guess but if you believe its a skill then giving funds to people who have a track record is one way to “maximize your ROI”.

Even if your goal is vaguely “impact”, which foundations are better at this in some quantitative way (still limited but better than nothing). Gates foundation for example talks about ‘saving a life with $1k’ as their yardstick. There’s a nonprofit that ranks impact by $, forgot the name of it…

text:
<@U5STGTB3J> The Nature of Order seems very interesting, going to read it so thanks for the heads up.

I intuitively have had somewhat similar ideas about order and the natural formation of systems, I struggle to even articulate most of my ideas on it, so this seems like a rabbit-hole waiting for me!

text:
After watching that talk on Datalog for 13 minutes, it wasn't hard to believe that datalog could be a better database than RDBMSs. But I'm biased - I've thought RDBMSs sucked for maybe 15 years or so. It sounds like this kind of DB will automatically optimize itself by automatically noticing when it would be useful to have a particular kind of index... or something?

Datalog has 5-tuples (entity, attribute, value, transaction_id, delete_flag). What is the id in [id, entity, attribute, value] and why is this used instead of the last two items in that tuple?

You said that "a list of id-entity-attribute-value tuples is enough to model any data." No doubt you could; it doesn't follow that you should though. In his talk Norbert Wojtowicz mentioned that there were cases when he wouldn't use Datalog for something.

When I looked at your code:
```identifier(0) : Attribute {
  Attribute.value-type = String
  comment = "..."
}```
I thought to myself, as I usually do, that this was similar to <http://loyc.net/les/|LES>. Have you heard of it? I'm just starting to ask people this question: (1) have your heard of LES or Loyc trees and (2) if you had heard about them, would you have considered using them (e.g. to make prototyping easier, or to separate the syntax of your language from its semantics)? Why or why not?
